content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 28, 2006

VIA U.S. MAIL AND FACSIMILE

Iris P. Yang
McDonough Holland & Allen PC
555 Capitol Mall, 9th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814-4692

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-198

Dear Ms. Yang:

This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance¹ on behalf of Vallejo City Councilmembers Tom Bartee, Gerald Davis, and Stephanie Gomes for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").² Please note that this letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; Gov. Code section 83114.)

You are requesting clarification of prior advice provided in the Soley Advice Letter, No. I-05-229 and the Gomes Advice Letter No. A-06-063 (which are incorporated herein as referenced), and you are also providing additional background information with regard to two sets of decisions that will be coming before the Vallejo City Council ("Council") and the Redevelopment Agency of the City of Vallejo ("Agency").

Regulation 18329 provides that the Commission may decline to provide advice if the requestor is seeking advice related to past conduct. In a letter dated October 30, 2006, you stated that Councilmembers Tom Bartee, Gerald Davis, and Stephanie Gomes have not participated in any decisions to date either on the Waterfront Project or the Plan Merger. However, in an April 10, 2006 request for advice written by Councilmember Stephanie Gomes, she stated, "As my home is not within 500 feet of the waterfront master plan boundary, I have been participating in discussions related to the waterfront

¹ Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

² Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 2

project." Because Ms. Gomes has participated in discussions regarding the Waterfront
Project, we decline to provide her with further advice.

In addition, the Vallejo City Council also took action on November 14, 2006, with
regard to the plan to merge three redevelopment areas in the city. Councilmembers
Bartee, Davis, and Gomes did not participate in this decision. However, because the city
council has taken action on this matter, we cannot offer advice regarding this decision.

QUESTION

May Councilmembers Tom Bartee and Gerald Davis participate in decisions
related to the Waterfront Project?

CONCLUSION

Councilmembers Bartee and Davis may participate in decisions regarding the
Waterfront Project so long as there is not a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on any of their economic interests.

FACTS

There are two sets of separate decisions before the Council and the Agency.
These decisions involve: (1) the Waterfront Project (which includes the Vallejo Station
Project and the Waterfront Project"), and (2) the merger and amendment of the Marina
Vista Redevelopment Plan, Waterfront Redevelopment Plan, and Vallejo Central
Redevelopment Plan (the "Plan Merger").

The Waterfront Project

The first set of decisions relates to the development of the Waterfront Project, a
mixed-use project along the Vallejo waterfront area. In October 2005, the Agency and
City Council approved a number of actions related to the Waterfront Project. These
included a General Plan Amendment, rezoning ordinance, Development Agreement
between the City and Callahan/deSilva Vallejo LLC, ("CDV"), and a Disposition and
Development Agreement between the Agency and CDV (the "Waterfront DDA"). The
Council and Agency also certified the Environmental Impact Report (the "EIR") prepared
in connection with the Waterfront Project.

The Waterfront Project is a development project that includes residential,
commercial, retail, open space, and park uses. It also includes the development of certain
public facilities including a public parking garage and multi-modal transit facilities (the
"Vallejo Station Project"). The Waterfront Project (including the Vallejo Station Project)
is located within two existing and adjacent redevelopment project areas – the Waterfront
Redevelopment Project Area and the Marina Vista Redevelopment Project Area.

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 3

The Waterfront DDA calls for the Agency to sell or lease certain property to CDV
for CDV to develop in phases. All but two of the properties to be conveyed to CDV are
owned either by the City or the Agency.

The Waterfront DDA states that it shall become effective upon the date that
certain Required Approvals have been approved; namely, certification of the EIR,
approval of the Development Agreement, General Plan Amendment, rezoning, and
Planned Development Master Plan. All of the required approvals have been approved
and the Waterfront DDA is now in effect.

You state in your letter that the effectiveness of the DDA is not dependent upon
the Plan Merger being approved. The final approvals of the Plan Merger are the
responsibility of the Planning Commission and City Council, not the Agency. If the Plan
Merger is not approved, the Marina Vista Redevelopment Plan will expire next year. If
either party is not able to fulfill certain obligations under the DDA as a result of the
expiration of the Marina Vista Redevelopment Plan or other changes effectuated by the
Proposed Plan Merger, there could be a partial termination of the DDA or renegotiation
of certain issues, if necessary; however, the DDA will still remain in effect.

The DDA "is specifically conditioned upon certain actions, including certification
of an environmental impact report and all other actions necessary to comply with the
California Environmental Quality Act, and amendment and merger of the Marina Vista,
Vallejo Waterfront and Vallejo Central Redevelopment Projects."¹

In December 2005 the Vallejo Waterfront Coalition filed a petition for writ of
mandate and injunctive relief against the City and Agency, asking the court to set aside
the various approvals related to the Waterfront Project. Since that time, the parties have
been engaged in settlement discussions, and it is expected that the City and Agency will
be considering the approval of a settlement agreement.

In our telephone conversation on November 7, 2006, you stated that none of the
current legal decisions related to the Waterfront Project, the Waterfront Project DDA, the
Waterfront Project litigation, or the settlement agreement involving the Waterfront
Project, would affect, or have any impact on, the Plan Merger. You also stated that the
only potential impact the Waterfront Project litigation might have on the Plan Merger is
that it "might call into question whether the EIR is adequate for the merger" because
there is one EIR for both the Waterfront Project and the Project Merger. You stated that
if such a challenge were successful against the EIR, "it might mean merger couldn't go
forward. But that would have to be a separate legal challenge. It's not part of existing
litigation."

¹ "The Report to the City Council on Proposed Amendments to the Redevelopment Plans
including the Merger of the Vallejo Waterfront, Vallejo Central, and Marina Vista Redevelopment
Projects," dated May 24, 2006.

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 4

In your letter dated October 16, 2006, you state that the Waterfront Project is a
distinct development project with distinct boundaries. (The Waterfront Project has
boundaries that are considerably smaller than the boundaries of the Plan Merger.) You
further state that "decisions about the Plan Merger are separate and distinct from those of
the Waterfront Project. While the two sets of decisions are related, they are not
'inextricably interrelated' as defined in Regulation 18709(b)."

In your request for advice, you stated that none of the various property interests
held by Councilmembers Tom Bartee and Gerald Davis are located within 500 feet of the
boundaries of the Waterfront Project.

The Plan Merger

Over the last two years, the City and Agency have been following procedures to
amend and merge the Marina Vista, Waterfront, and Vallejo Central Redevelopment
Plans. The project areas designated by the three redevelopment plans are contiguous to
each other.

Several of the preliminary decisions regarding the proposed Plan Merger have
been made. These steps involve the setting of a joint Agency-City Council public
hearing, approval of certain resolutions, including the certification of the Environmental
Impact Report ("EIR"), and adoption of three ordinances to amend the three
Redevelopment Plans. The EIR prepared in connection with the Plan Merger is the same
EIR that was prepared for the Waterfront Project; in other words, the same document
contained the environmental analysis for two projects.

You stated in your letter dated October 16, 2006, that you assume that
Councilmembers Bartee and Davis have a conflict of interest in the Plan Merger
decisions because they own real property within 500 feet of the boundaries of the Plan
Merger, (which is larger than the boundaries of the Waterfront Project).

As mentioned previously, Councilmembers Bartee and Davis did not participate
in the November 14, 2006 decision regarding the Plan Merger.

ANALYSIS

Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition: Section 87100 prohibits any public official
from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether
an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 5

Steps 1 & 2: Are Councilmembers Bartee and Davis "public officials"
making, participating in making, or influencing governmental decisions?

As members of the Vallejo City Council, Councilmembers Bartee and Davis are
public officials under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, they may not make,
participate in making, or otherwise use their official position to influence any decisions
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of their economic
interests. The councilmembers will be called upon to consider whether the City should
approve or disapprove the adoption of certain proposals related to the Waterfront Project.
Therefore, they will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision. 4

Step 3: Do Councilmembers Bartee and Davis have potentially disqualifying
economic interests?

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(e); regulation
18703.4);

4
If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (including city council members) and he or
she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as
well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5, subdivision (b)(1)(B), on the
record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the
discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences, and speaking as a
member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions
(c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.) Since Mr. Bartee and Mr. Davis are city council members (a position
enumerated in section 87200) these requirements apply to them.

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 6

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (section 87103;
regulation 18703.5).

Real Property Interests: You stated in your letter of October 16, 2006, that
Councilmembers Bartee and Davis have various real property interests (as shown on a
map you included with your request for advice), none of which are within 500 feet of the
Waterfront Project. The councilmembers have an economic interest in the above real
property, provided that they have a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more.

Other Economic Interests: Your request does not present any facts indicating that
Councilmember Davis has any other economic interests. However, the previous request
for advice (Soley, supra) on the councilmember's behalf, indicated that various
residential properties in which Councilmember Davis has an ownership interest are used
as residential rentals, thereby raising the issue that Councilmember Davis may have a
source of income economic interest, under section 87103 (c) and regulation 18703.3, in
one or more of the tenants of these properties, as well as a business interest under section
87103 (a) and (d) and regulation 18703.1.³

Personal Finances: Councilmembers Bartee and Davis also have an economic
interest in their personal finances and those of their immediate family. (Section 87103;
regulation 18703.5.) A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic
interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of
the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing.

Your letter does not mention any impact on Councilmembers Bartee's and
Davis's personal finances as the result of the upcoming decisions, so we do not further
analyze this interest. However, please note that a conflict of interest may arise if it is
reasonably foreseeable that any of the above decisions will have an effect on the
councilmembers' personal finances.

Step 4: Is the Economic Interest Directly or Indirectly Involved in the
Governmental Decision?

Real Property Interests: Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a
public official has an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if
any of the following apply:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part
of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the

³ The various properties identified in the Soley letter are as follows: 525/527 Maine Street; 503
Benecia Road; 352/352½ Thomas Avenue, and 44 Woodrow Avenue, all in the city of Vallejo. None of
the properties are within 500 feet of the boundaries of the Waterfront Project. Only one of these properties
525/527 Maine Street, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of any of the redevelopment areas.

File No. I-06-198
Page No. 7

proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision . . .

"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property . . . .

"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an interest.

"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in
which the official has an interest.

"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) or the redevelopment area.

"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."

Indirectly Involved Properties: Because Councilmember Bartee's and Davis's
properties are not located within 500 feet of the boundaries or proposed boundaries of the
Waterfront Project, which is the subject of the governmental decision, they appear
indirectly involved.⁶ (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).)

⁶ A public official's real property is also deemed to be directly involved in a governmental
decision if "the decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage
or similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services." (Regulation 18704.2(a)(6).) You have not provided any information regarding new or improved
services that may benefit the councilmembers' property as the result of the project. However, please note if
the Waterfront Project decision will result in such improvements, or will add new services to the area
affecting the councilmembers' properties, the properties will be deemed directly involved in, and thus, the
financial effect of the governmental decision on the councilmembers' property would be presumed
material.

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 8

Source of Income and Business Entities:

You do not provide any facts to indicate whether any business entity, or source of
income in which Councilmember Davis has an economic interest would be directly or
indirectly involved the Waterfront Project.

Regulation 18704.1 states that a person, including business entities and sources of
income is directly involved in a governmental decision if that person, either directly or by
an agent: "(1) initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request; or (2) is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of the proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the subject person."

If a source of income or business entity is not directly involved in the
governmental decision, it is deemed to be indirectly involved.

Step 5: Applicable Materiality Standard

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.

Indirectly Involved Real Property: Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public
official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the official has an
interest. Examples of these specific circumstances include circumstances where the
decision affects:

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic interest;

"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 9

"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."

Because the councilmembers' properties are located more than 500 feet from the
boundaries of the Waterfront Project, the subject of the governmental decision, their
properties are deemed indirectly involved in this decision.

**Business Entity:** Regulation 18705.1 provides the materiality standard for
economic interests in business entities that would apply to Councilmember Davis' rental
business.

**Directly Involved Business Entities:** For a business entity that is directly involved
in a governmental decision, regulation 18705.1(b)(1) states that unless the exception in
subdivision (b)(2) applies, "the financial effects of a governmental decision on a business
entity which is directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be material.
The presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect on the business entity.

**Indirectly Involved Business Entities:** Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the
materiality standard for indirectly involved business entities. For a relatively small
business, the standard is provided under subsection (c)(4):

"(4) ... the financial effect of a governmental decision on a business entity is
material if it is reasonable foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $ 20,000 or
more; or

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $ 5,000 or more; or

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $ 20,000 or more."

**Source of Income:** Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standard for an
economic interest in persons who are sources of income to Councilmember Davis.

**Directly Involved Source of Income:** For a source of income that is directly
involved in a governmental decision, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on that
person is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 10

Indirectly Involved Source of Income: The financial effect of a governmental
decision is deemed material with respect to an individual who is a source of income to an
official if any of the following applies:

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or
more; or

(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that
is considered material under Title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
18705.2 (b)." (Regulation 18705.3 (b)(3).)

Step 6: Foreseeability

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood of that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.)

Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing
advice (In re Oglesby, supra), therefore, the foreseeability of a particular financial effect
is a determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the
public official.

For indirectly involved real property interests (other than leaseholds) the financial
effect of a governmental decision on the real property is presumed not to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in
which the public official has an economic interest. However, we have advised that in
order to rebut this presumption of nonmateriality, the effect of the governmental decision
must result in a clearly distinguishable and substantial effect on the official's real
property. (Berger Advice Letter, No. A-05-054.)

You have not presented any facts rebutting this presumption with regard to the
Waterfront Project. Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing
advice (In re Oglesby, supra), it is ultimately up to Councilmembers Bartee and Davis to
decide whether their facts are sufficient to rebut this presumption. If it is not rebutted,
they may participate in decisions relating to the Waterfront Project.

File No. I-06-198
Page No. 11

In addition, Councilmember Davis must also take into account his other economic
interests. We do not have any facts to analyze the potential financial effects of the
Waterfront project on his rental business, or any of his tenants, or sources of income.
Therefore, Councilmember Davis must determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the materiality standards for each economic interest will be met, and therefore,
whether a conflict of interest will arise from any of these interests.

Segmentation of Decisions

In addition, you stated that none of the current decisions related to the Waterfront
Project, the Waterfront Project DDA, the Waterfront Project litigation, or the settlement
agreement involving the Waterfront Project would affect, or have any impact on, the Plan
Merger -- a decision in which (based on your assumption) the councilmembers have a
conflict of interest.

Generally, decisions are analyzed independently to determine if there will be a
foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest. (In re Owen
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) The Commission has advised that "[u]nder certain
circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other
related decisions provided that the official's participation does not affect the decision in
which he or she has a conflict of interest." (In re Owen, supra.)

However, circumstances can arise where the decisions on separate projects are too
"inextricably related" to be considered separately and, in such cases, a public official's
conflict of interest in one decision will disqualify him or her from participating in the
other decisions. Decisions are inextricably interrelated where, among other things, one
decision is a necessary condition precedent or condition subsequent required for another. (See for
example, Stone Advice Letter, No. A-06-007; Ball Advice Letter, No. A-98-124.)
Decisions are also considered inextricably interrelated when the result of one decision
will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision.
(Regulation 18709 (b).)

If decisions are not inextricably linked, a public official will not have to be
disqualified if those decisions can be properly segmented. The circumstances under
which a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other related
decisions are set forth in regulation 18709(a) and (b):

"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following
conditions apply:

"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

File No. 1-06-198
Page No. 12

"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from
the other decisions;

"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's
participation in any way; and

"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified.

"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when
the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the
result of another decision."

According to the facts you have presented in the May 24, 2006 "Report to the
City Council" on the proposed amendments to the redevelopment plans, the Waterfront
Project DDA is "specifically conditioned upon certain actions, including … amendment
and merger of the Marina Vista, Vallejo Waterfront and Vallejo Central Redevelopment
Projects." (Emphasis added.)

You also state in your letter of October 16, 2006, that "if the Plan Merger is not
approved, the Marina Vista Redevelopment Plan will expire next year." In addition, if
either party is unable to fulfill certain obligations under the DDA as a result of the
expiration of the Marina Vista Redevelopment Plan or other changes effectuated by the
Proposed Plan Merger, there could be a partial termination of the DDA or renegotiation
of certain issues, if necessary; however, the DDA will still remain in effect.

Therefore, decisions involving the Plan Merger may determine, affirm, nullify, or
alter the result of the Waterfront Project. However, based on your facts, the reverse does
not appear to be true.

Under your facts, it does not appear that the Waterfront Project decisions
effectively affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the Plan Merger. Therefore, the
Waterfront Project decisions do not appear inextricably interrelated to decisions
involving the Plan Merger, although the two appear related.

Therefore, if circumstances change regarding the Waterfront Project decisions as
it relates to the Plan Merger (for instance, if future decisions involving the Waterfront
Project affect or effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the Plan Merger), you may
wish to seek further advice.

File No. I-06-198
Page No. 13


Steps 7 & 8: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.

The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101; regulation 18708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
your situation, therefore we do not address them here.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.


Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

ER:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\06-198

sections.question:
May Councilmembers Tom Bartee and Gerald Davis participatein decisions
related to the Waterfront Project?
sections.conclusion:
Councilmembers Bartee and Davis may parlicipate in decisions regarding the
Waterfront Project so long as there is not a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on any of their economic interests.

certain Required Approvals have been approved; namely, certification of the EIR,
approval of the Development Agreement, General Plan Amendment, rezoning, and
Planned Development Master Plan. A11 of the required approvals have been approved
and the Waterfront DDA is now in effect.
You state in your letter that the effectiveness of the DDA is not dependent upon
the Plan Merger being approved. The final approvals of the Plan Merger are the
responsibility of the Planning Commission and City Council, not the Agency. If the plan
Merger is not approved, the Marina Vista Redevelopment Plan will expire nlxt year. If
either party is not able to fuIfilI certain obligations under the DDA as a result of the
expiration of the Marina Vista Redevelopment Plan or other changes effectuated by the
Proposed Plan Merger, there could be a partial termination of the DDA or renegotiation
of certain issues, if necessary; however, the DDA will still remain in effect.
The DDA "is specifically conditioned upon certain actions, including certification
of an environmental impact report and all other actions necessary to comply with the
California Environmental Quality Act, and amendment and merge^r of the Marina Vista,
vallejo waterfront and vallejo central Redevelopment projects.,,3
In Decembet 2005 the Vallejo Waterfront Coalition filed a petition for writ of
mandate and injunctive relief against the City and Agency, asking the court to set aside
the various approvals related to the Waterfront Project. Since thal time, the parties have
been engaged in settlement discussions, and it is expected that the City and Agency will
be considering the approval of a settlement agreement.
In our telephone conversation on November 7,2006, you stated that none of the
current decisions related to the Waterfront Project, the Waterfront project DDA, the
Waterfront Project litigation, or the settlement agreement involving thl Waterfront
Project, would affecl, or have any impact on, the Plan Merger. you also stated that the
only potential impact the Waterfront Project litigation might have on the plan Merger is
that it "might call into question whether the EIR is adequate for the merger,, because
there is one EIR for both the Waterlront Project and the Project Merger. you stated that
if such a challenge were successful against the EIR, "It might mean *..g., couldn,t go
forward. But that would have to be a separate legal challenge. It's not part of existilg
litigation."
' "Th. Report to the City Council on Proposed Amendments to the Redevelopment plans
including the Merger of the Vallejo Waterfront, Vallejo Central, and Marina Vista Redevelopment
Projects," dated May 24,2006.
In your letter dated October 76,2006, you state that the Waterfront project is a
distinct development project with distinct boundaries. (The Waterfront project has
boundaries that are considerably smaller than the boundaries of the plan Merger.) you
fuither state that "decisions about the Plan Merger are separate and distinct from those of
the Waterfront Project. While the two sets of decisions are related, they are not
'inextricably interrelated' as defined in Regulation 1g709(b).,,
In your request for advice, you stated that none ofthe various property interests
held by Councilmembers Tom Bartee and Gerald Davis are located *ittin 500 feet of the
boundaries of the Waterfront Project.
The PIan Merger
Over the last two years, the City and Agency have been following procedures to
amend and merge the Marina Vista, Waterfront, and Vallejo Central Redlvelopment
Plans. The project areas designated by the three redevelopment plans are contiguous to
each other.
Several of the preliminary decisions regarding the proposed plan Merger have
been made. The next steps involve the setting of a joint Agency-City Councif public
hearing, approval of certain resolutions, including the certification oithe Environmental
Impact Report ("EIR"), and adoption of three ordinances to amend the three
Redevelopment Plans, The EIR prepared in connection with the plan Merger is the same
EIR that was prepared for the Waterfront Project; in other words, the samJdocument
contained the environmental analysis for two projects.
You stated in your letter dated october 16,2006, that you assume that
Councilmembers Bartee and Davis have a conflict of interest in the plan Merger
decisions because they own real property within 500 feet of the boundaries of-the plan
Merger, (which is larger than the boundaries of the waterfront project).
As mentioned previously, Councilmembers Bartee and Davis did not participate
in the November 14,2006 decision regarding the plan Merger.
sections.analysis:
Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition: Section 87100 prohibits any public official
frorn making, participating in making, or otherwise using hir o. t ". oti"iut position to
influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether
an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision.
(Regulation i 8700(bXl)-(8) )
Steps I & 2: Are Councilmembers Bartee and Davis o.public officials,,
making, participating in making, or influencing governmental decisions?
As members of the Vallejo City Council, Councilmembers Bartee and Davis are
public officials under the Act. (Section B2o4g.) consequently, they may not make,
participate in making, or otherwise use their official position to influenc e any decisions
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of their economic
interests. The councilmembers will be called upon to consider whether the City should
approve or disapprove the adoption of certain proposals related to the Waterfront project.
Therefore, they will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision. 
a
Step 3: Do Councilmembers Bartee and Davis have potentially disqualifying
economic interests?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateial
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(a); regulation 1s703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management (section g71 03(d); regulation 1 g703. itull,
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation lg703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to thl decision (section
871 03(c); regulation 1 8703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$360 or more within l2 months prior to the decision (section 87103(eiielulation
18703.$;
a
a
a
a
a If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (including city council members) and he or
she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, t"hen-he or she must: (1j immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, oraily identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as
well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation lSl02.5,subdivision (b)(1j(e), on the
record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration'oiihe
discussion atd/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences, und rpeukirg us u
member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation lgT02.i,subdivisions
(c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105). Since Mr. Bartee and Mr. Davis are city council members (a position
enumerated in section 87200) these requirements apply to them.


o
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate famiiy -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (sectio n 81103:
regulation 1 8703.5).
Real Property Interests: You stated in your letter of October 16, 2006, that
Councilmembers Bartee and Davis have various real property interests (as shown on a
map you included with your request for advice), none of which are within 500 feet of the
Waterfront Project. The councilmembers have an economic interest in the above real
property, provided that they have a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more.
Other Economic Interests: Your request does not present any facts indicating that
Councilmember Davis has any other economic interests. However, the previous request
for advice (Soley, supra) on the councilmember's behalf, indicated that various
residential properties in which Councilmember Davis has an ownership interest are used
as residential rentals, thereby raising the issue that Councilmember Davis may have a
source of income economic interest, under section 87103 (c) and regulation 18703.3, in
one or more of the tenants of these properties, as well as a business interest under section
87103 (a) and (d) and regulation 18703.1.5
Personal Finances: Councilmembers Bartee and Davis also have an economic
interest in their personal finances and those of their immediate family. (Section 87103;
regulation 18703.5.) A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic
interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of
the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing.
Your letter does not mention any impact on Councilmembers Bartee's and
Davis's personal finances as the result of the upcoming decisions, so we do not further
analyze this interest. However, please note that a conflict of interest may arise if it
reasonably foreseeable that any of the above decisions will have an effect on the
councilmembers' personal finances.
Step 4: Is the Economic Interest Directly or Indirectly Involved in the
Governmental Decision ?
Real Propertlr Interests: Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a
public official has an economic interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if
any of the following apply:
"( 1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part
of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the
5 The various properties identified tn the Soley letter are as foilows: 525/521 Maine Street; 503
Benecia Road; 35213521,/zThomas Avenue, and 44 Woodrow Avenue, all in the city of Valle;o. None of
the properties are within 500 feet of the boundaries of the Waterfront Project. Only one of these properties
5251527 Maine Street, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of any of the redevelopment areas.
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision ... .
"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property ... .
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authoizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in
which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) or the redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of; or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
seryices."
Indirectly Involved Properties: Because Councilmember Bartee's and Davis's
properties are not located within 500 feet of the boundaries or proposed boundaries of the
Waterfront Project, which is the subject of the goverrrmental decision, they appear
i n d i r e c t ly involved. 6 (Regul ation 1 S7 04.2({ Qj.)
6 A public official's real property is also deemed to be directly involved in a governmental
decision if "the decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage
or similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services." (Regulation 1810a.2@)(6).) You have not provided any information regarding new or improved
services that may benefit the councilmembers' properry as the result of the project. Flowever, please note if
the Waterfront Project decision will result in such impiovements, or will add new services to the area
affecting the councilmembers' properties, the properties will be deemed directly involved and, thus, the
financial effect of the govemmental decision on the councilmembers' property would be presumed
material.
Source of Income and Business Entities:
You do not provide any facts to indicate whether any business entity, or source of
income in which Councilmember Davis has an economic interest would be directly or
indirectly involved the Waterfront Project.
Regulation 18704.1 states that aperson, including business entities and sources of
income is directly involved in a governmental decision if that person, either directly or by
an agent: "(1) initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request; or (2) is a named party in, or is the subject
of the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or thqofficial,s agency. A
person is the subject of the proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
apptoval, denial or revocation of any license, permit or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the subject person."
If a source of income or business entity is not directly involved in the
governmental decision, it is deemed to be indirectly involved.
Step 5: Applicable Materiality Standard
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be matenaT,depending o, th" nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
Indirectly Involved Real Propertlz: Regulation 1g705.2(b)(1) provides that the
ftnancial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved
in the governmental decision is presttmed not to be material. This prestmption may be
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the goveinmental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public
official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the official has an
interest. Examples of these specific circumstances include circumstances where the
decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic interest,
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy,intensityof use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.,,
Because the councilmembers' properties are located more than 500 feet from the
boundaries of the Waterfront Project, the subject of the governmental decision, their
properties are deemed indirectly involved in this decision.
Business Entify: Regulation 18705.1 provides the materiality standard for
economic interests in business entities that would apply to Councilmember Davis, rental
business.
Directly Involved Business Entities: For a business entity that is directly involved
in a governmental decision, regulation 18705.1(bXl) states that unless the exception in
subdivision (b)(2) applies, "the financial effects of a govemmental decision on a business
entity which is directly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be material.
The presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonabiy foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect on the business entitv.
Invol
Enti
Regulation 1 8705. 1 (c) provides the
materiality standard for indirectly involved business entities. For a relatively small
business, the standard is provided under subsection (c)(4)
"(4) . .- the financial effect of a govemmental decision on a business entitv is
material if it is reasonable foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $ 20.000 or
more; or
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses-for a
fiscal year in the amount of $ 5,000 or more; or
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $ 20,000 or more.,,
Source of Income: Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standard for an
economic interest in persons who are sources of income to Councilmember Davis.
Directl-v Involved Source of Income: For a source of income that is directly
involved in a govemmental decision, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on that
person is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)
Indirectly Involved Source of Income: The financial effect of a governmental
decision is deemed material with respect to an individuai who is a source of income to an
official if any of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $ 1,000 or
more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that
is considered material under Title2, Califomia Code of Regulations, section
18705.2 (b)." (Regulation 18705.3 (bX3).)
Step 6: Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable . (Ibid.)
Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing
advice (In re Oglesby, sltpra), therefore, the foreseeability of a particular financial effect
is a determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the
public official.
For indirectly involved reai properly interests (other than leaseholds) the financial
effect of a governmental decision on the real property is presumed not to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in
which the public official has an economic interest. However, we have advised that in
order to rebut this presumption of nonmateriality, the effect of the governmental decision
must result in a clearly distinguishable and substantial effect on the official's real
property. (Berger Advice Letter, No. A-05-054.)
You have not presented any facts rebutting this presumption with regard to the
Waterfront Project. Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing
advice (In re Oglesby, sttpra), it is ultimately up to Councilmembers Bartee and Davis to
decide whether their facts are sufficient to rebut this presumption. if it is not rebutted,
they may participate in decisions relating to the Waterfront Project.
In addition, Councilmember Davis must also take into account his other economic
interests. We do not have any facts to analyze the potenti al financial effects of the
Waterfront project on his rental business, or any of his tenants, or sources of income.
Therefore, Councilmember Davis must determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the materiality standards for each economic interest will be met, and iherefore,
whether a conflict of interest will arise from any of these interests.
S
of Decisions
In addition, you stated that none of the current decisions related to the Waterfront
Project, the Waterfront Project DDA, the Waterfront Project litigation, or the settlement
agreement involving the Waterfront Project would affect, or have any impact on, the plan
Merger -- a decision in which (based on your assumption) the councilmembers have a
conflict of interest.
Generally, decisions arc analyzed independently to determine if there will be a
foreseeable material financial effect on an official's frnancial interest. (In re Owen
(1976) 2 FPPC ops. 77.) The commission has advised that,,[u]nder certain
circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision *y participate in other
related decisions provided that the official's participation does noi#fect the decision in
which he or she has a conflict of interest.,, (In re Owen, supra.)
However, circumstances can arise where the decisions on separate projects are too
"inextricably related" to be considered separately and, in such cases, a public tffi"iul,,
conflict of interest in one decision will disqualify him or her 1rom participating in the
other decisions. Decisions are inextricably interrelated where, among other things, one
decision is a necessary condition precedent or condition subsequent for another. (See, for
example, stone Advice Letter, No. 4-06-007; Ball Advice Lettlr, No. A-9g_124.)
Decisions are also considered inextricably interrelated when the result of one decision
will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision.
(Regulation I 8709 (b).)
If decisions are not inextricably linked, a public official will not have to be
disqualified if those decisions can be properly segmented. The circumstances under
which a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other related
decisions are set forth in regulation 18709(a) and (b):
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following
conditions apply:
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financi al interest is segmented from
the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualifiLd official,s
parlicipation in any way; and
"(4) once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made,
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official *u, airq,rulified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextri cablyinterrelated, when
the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, ,rilify, or alter the
result of another decision.,,
According to the facts you have presented in the May 24,2oo6,.Report to the
City council" on the proposed amendments to the redevelopment plans, thJwaterfront
Project DDA is "specifically conditioned upon certain actio^ns, lnctuaing ... amendment
and merger o.f the Marina vista, vallejo waterfront and vallejo centri Redevelopment
Projects." (Emphasis added.)
You also state in your letter of october 16,2o06,that "if the plan Merger is not
approved, the Marina VistaRedevelopment Plan will expire next year.,, In addition, if
either party is not able to fulfill certain obligations undei the DDA as a result of the
expiration of the Marina vista Redevelopment Plan or other changes effectuated by the
Proposed Plan Merger, there could be apartial termination of the boa or renegotiation
of certain issues, if necessary; however, ih" DDA wil still remain in effect.
Therefore, decisions involving the Plan Merger may determine, affirm, nullify, or
alter the result of the waterfront Project. However,lased on your facts, the reverse does
not appear to be true.
Under your fa_ctq it does not appear that the Waterfront project decisions
effectively affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the plan Merger. Therefore, the
waterfront Project decisions do not appear inextricably interrelated to decisions
involving the Plan Merger, although the two appear reiated.
Therefore, if circumstances change regarding the Waterfront project decisions as
it relates to the Plan Merger (for instance, if future decisions involving the waterfront
Project affect or effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the plan Merger), you may
wish to seek further advice.
Steps 7 & 8: public Generaily and Legarly Required participation
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official,s participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. lsection 87101; regulation 1g70g.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
your situation, therefore we do not address thern here.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmembers Tom Bartee and Gerald Davis participatein decisions
related to the Waterfront Project?

CONCLUSION: Councilmembers Bartee and Davis may parlicipate in decisions regarding the
Waterfront Project so long as there is not a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on any of their economic interests.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 November 28, 2006 VIA U.S. MAIL AND FACSIMILE Iris P. Yang McDonough Holland & Allen PC 555 Capitol Mall, 9th Floor Sacramento, CA 95814-4692 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-198 Dear Ms. Yang: This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance¹ on behalf of Vallejo City Councilmembers Tom Bartee, Gerald Davis, and Stephanie Gomes for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").² Please note that this letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; Gov. Code section 83114.) You are requesting clarification of prior advice provided in the Soley Advice Letter, No. I-05-229 and the Gomes Advice Letter No. A-06-063 (which are incorporated herein as referenced), and you are also providing additional background information with regard to two sets of decisions that will be coming before the Vallejo City Council ("Council") and the Redevelopment Agency of the City of Vallejo ("Agency"). Regulation 18329 provides that the Commission may decline to provide advice if the requestor is seeking advice related to past conduct. In a letter dated October 30, 2006, you stated that Councilmembers Tom Bartee, Gerald Davis, and Stephanie Gomes have not participated in any decisions to date either on the Waterfront Project or the Plan Merger. However, in an April 10, 2006 request for advice written by Councilmember Stephanie Gomes, she stated, "As my home is not within 500 feet of the waterfront master plan boundary, I have been participating in discussions related to the waterfront ¹ Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).) ² Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. 1-06-198 Page No. 2 project." Because Ms. Gomes has participated in discussions regarding the Waterfront Project, we decline to provide her with further advice. In addition, the Vallejo City Council also took action on November 14, 2006, with regard to the plan to merge three redevelopment areas in the city. Councilmembers Bartee, Davis, and Gomes did not participate in this decision. However, because the city council has taken action on this matter, we cannot offer advice regarding this decision. QUESTION May Councilmembers Tom Bartee and Gerald Davis participate in decisions related to the Waterfront Project? CONCLUSION Councilmembers Bartee and Davis may participate in decisions regarding the Waterfront Project so long as there is not a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of their economic interests. FACTS There are two sets of separate decisions before the Council and the Agency. These decisions involve: (1) the Waterfront Project (which includes the
